transcript: [music] Hello everybody. It's Eddie Ardeni. Today is actually Sunday, July 12th, and it's uh 10:20 p.m. Uh I'm uh pre-recording our weekly webinar. got another commitment on on Monday. So, I apologize, but we're not going to have a a Q&A, but I did want to uh follow up and and all the information that's occurred over the past week or so. Um, and even on Sunday, we're we're seeing there's uh news related that'll impact the market. uh in particular we have um a uh continuation of the war between the US and Iran. We got shooting war going on. U the president claims that this trade is open. uh in that shooting war, the apparently the the the US military uh took some uh heavy duty uh action against uh targets in the street of Harmuz on the Iranian side uh to defang them to reduce their ability to threaten the tra transit of commercial vessels including oil ship oil laden ships uh through through the straight. Uh, let me just move this uh mic over here. Sometimes I forget this mic, but it's a good old Yeti mic, so it uh picks things up. Uh, but um anyways, uh I forgot to mention, we had a nice weekend here. Weather is great, but uh as I'm sure you all know here in the States, we got another so-called heat dome coming uh starting on Tuesday and then Wednesday and Thursday. So, not looking forward to that. Um question is uh with regards to what we do for a living is should we be looking forward to the earnings season uh coming up here uh this week we're going to have the major banks reporting and uh then it just keeps on going uh for a few weeks until they all report their second quarters. Uh uh some of you may have seen um the uh webinar I did last week with Nick Re of uh earning scout and Nick said that the 20 companies that have already already reported everybody thinks that the earning season only starts with the banks but it's actually already started with 20 companies reporting and he says that's usually a pretty good indic leading indicator for what the rest of the quarter season, the quarterly earning season will look like and he says that the earning story looks pretty solid. Some of that is Micron of course but even without Micron the earnings uh the leading indicators uh for the uh earning season look look quite good. Um here's my concern with regards to the earning season and that is let's call it great expectations. Uh the uh expectations for earnings are very very strong. Uh double digit increases. Uh 20 you know something in the low 20% year-over-year is what people are expecting for the third uh second third and fourth quarters. When I say people I mean industry analysts and u it is industry analyst consensus expectations that um gets into the market. That's what the market discounts when we do PE time Z. Uh the market's not discounting what I think earnings are going to do, what you think earnings are going to do. They're discounting what analysts think earnings are going to do. And then it's up to investors to decide how much of a multiple they want to pay for those expectations. And as we've seen over the past several months, earnings expectations have been extremely exuberant. And that's because actual earnings were much stronger than expected in the first quarter and that led to upward revisions in the second, third and fourth quarters. Uh more traditionally in the past what what happened is as you u as we get up to an earnings uh reporting season, analysts actually lower their numbers. Uh because the companies that uh aren't going to report very good results get the word out to the analysts. they they want to have this thing come out as a shocker um just when they report. So they kind of give the analysts a hint that things aren't going to be as good as they were thinking. So they they lower their numbers. On the other hand, companies that are going to report uh blockbuster results probably think the other way around and that is uh let's shock the market by how much better we've done than was widely expected and that'll have a very positive impact on our stock price. And uh I think that explains why uh you tend to see more uh downdrafts in earnings expectations uh going into earning seasons and then lo and behold when you actually get into the earning season more often than not you get upside surprises for for the market. This time around it's very unlikely we're going to have any upside surprises given how strong the expectations are uh for the second quarter. So what if we uh don't uh meet those expectations? Well, the market will probably sell off on that kind of news. What if we just meet exuberant expectations? Well, the market might still sell off to the extent that they've all those expectations have already been discounted in the market. Uh now, of course, uh these sell-offs could very well and probably would be uh buying opportunities. uh at the same time they may also uh lead to uh more of a rotation in the market away from uh some of the areas where earnings expectations have been particularly ex exuberant um mean basically S&P 500 growth and uh into more value areas where uh traditionally earnings expectations don't get as hyped up as they do in growth. Uh, so we may actually get more rotation as a result of this earning season. Anyways, I I think we're still in a bull market. I don't think whatever results we get are going to suddenly create a bare market. I don't even think they'll create a correction. And as we've seen of late, uh, anytime you do get a pullback in the market, dip buyers do do come in. Uh on another front that's relevant to the market, uh Fed Chair Kevin Wars is going to be testifying before Congress uh this this week uh two two days in a row and u he will be asked lots of questions and I won't be surprised if he said uh you know sen senator or or cong or uh congressman or congresswoman Uh I'm I I'm not going to answer that because we have a task force working on that. Uh he set up five task forces uh to work out work on various issues of that. He'd like to reform the uh the Fed. Um it's not clear exactly what he he will do, but uh he clearly wants to have some cover uh by having appointed these committees. Looks like each committee or task force has three people on it and I think they have to do with data. What what should the Fed do about data? What data should be they be looking at? Should they be trying to find more uh real time data kind of like we had during during COVID? There were a lot of indicators where people were looking at almost on a daily basis. Um I don't know why those kind of disappeared. maybe uh economists figure that uh reservations at the restaurants uh once you're in normal times are not going to do much and so we we don't really pay much attention to to that kind of data. Uh but uh there's certainly plenty of weekly data that we all pay attention to and the Fed does. I'm I'm not really convinced that we have a problem with uh new finding new data series. I think what we need to do is improve the quality of the data series that we already have and particularly in in the employment front uh and generally speaking in the consumer front and the inflation front um there's a task force for that as well and uh you know Borsch would like the task force to study uh the impact of AI on productivity and helping to bring inflation down. uh at the same time there's a whole new concern now that AI before it uh turns out to be disinflationary by boosting productivity that in the short term in the here and now AI is boosting memory de uh chip demand uh and and prices that it's boosting u the demand for construction workers uh and manufacturing workers so that might put some upward pressure on on wages Um, and so, uh, all of a sudden we got a concern that AI might be inflationary. And by the way, the minutes, the FOMC minutes, the June FOMC minutes that came out last week, uh, had, uh, quite an extensive discussion of AI. And I guess because Wars isn't giving any forward guidance, there wasn't much discussion about the productivity enhancing uh aspects of AI in the minutes, but there was a lot of discussion about the inflationary consequences in the short run of of AI. So that's something that u seems to be uh uh quite quite uh there seems to be a growing consensus among Fed officials and that's because the data is is confirming that we're seeing some some of the CPI and PPI indicators that are relevant uh to inflation uh are definitely heating up. Uh meanwhile the labor market indicators uh are are looking pretty good quite good. Um and as a result of that uh the uh late the the pivot that the FOMC made in June uh to go from an easing stance to a hawkish stance or tightening stance is totally appropriate. And the only surprise back then was that even Kevin Worsh signed up and joined the consensus and reiterated that the FOMC's uh main priority right now is in fact to uh uh provide price stability to bring price stability and uh Wars u you know acknowledged uh clearly upfront that the Fed has not hit its 2% inflation target for over five years and he seemed to want to get the the markets to believe that he's really going to bring inflation down. So for all his talk about uh he's he's not going to provide forward guidance, he gave a lot of forward guidance, uh the Fed is no longer in a uh uh easing stance. It's in a tightening stance. uh the Fed is uh more committed than ever to bringing back uh price stability. And um meanwhile, while he maybe wants to see less communications coming out of the Fed, his his view is the market should make up their own mind based on data where interest rates should be. And he'd like to use that information as data uh to uh drive monetary policy. But the world isn't that that simple, of course. And the markets are going to be looking for any signs, any hints uh of what Fed Fed thinking is is is going through. And um I don't know, it's possible Kevin Worsh will tell all the presidents of the uh various regional feds that they shouldn't be talking anymore, making speeches, but I doubt that he'll actually make a make a ruling like that. I think you'd get too much uh push back. And meanwhile, these people like to talk to the press and have their opinions uh bandied about uh in in the media. So, I'm sure we're still going to get plenty of guidance. There'll still be issues about the whether the dot plot is going to exist. Uh I mean, you know, I'm I'm very biased. I'm I'm a Fed watcher, and to the extent that I'm a Fed watcher, the the more I have to to write about the better. Uh, but I have I acknowledge I've I've called them the Federal Open Mouth Committee. Uh, obviously implying that I even I think that they're talking too much. But again, as a Fed watcher, the more they talk, the more I got I have to write about and uh I'm a content provider and and so are they. If but if suddenly they climb up then that could mean that us poor Fed watchers are going to be left to our own devices and we may just have to do what Worsh wants us to do just focus on the data uh and uh influence the markets as we think is appropriate and then the Fed will see see what the markets are saying and respond accordingly. I mean, personally, I don't really have a problem with the Fed uh taking its cues from the bond vigilantes. Uh right now, the bond vigilantes are sort of um in in a kind of a neutral stance on their own, waiting to see what the Fed does. But uh I think I think the bond vigilantes uh might have pushed yields higher but for the fact that Kevin Walsh uh has come across as as a hawk after all as opposed to a dove which is what it sounded like when he's he was interviewing for the job. Um well let's let's look at some charts that are relevant to all this as we typically do. Um let's go to the sharing button here and here we go. Let's start out with uh Warser's uh tasks or or to-do list and um again uh during his press conference after the June meeting of the FOMC uh just about every other question uh that uh from the reporters that uh basically involved forward guidance, he uh kind of fluffed off and said uh we have we will have task for that task force for that and and now they they actually do and it uh will these task force will give the results by the end of the year uh which is uh u still a ways off and u gives us some things to uh to talk about until we we get the task force reports and then we'll see how they actually get implemented. Uh meanwhile uh with regards to that AI story uh there's there's evidence that AI is uh boosting the economy. We know that boosting employment. So if it's doing that in terms of the dual mandate the Fed u has to give less concern to the unemployment rate. By by the way, you know, when the Fed eased in 2024 and by 100 basis points and last year by uh 75 basis points, those easing moves were kind of sold as insurance policies to make sure that the labor market doesn't kind of implode. And now that the labor market's actually not imploding and showing some uh resilience, I know there's some controversy because of the latest payroll employment numbers, but overall uh the labor market indicators still look like it's hanging in there pretty well all things considered. Um so doesn't that imply that those insurance policies really weren't necessary? And if that if that's the case, shouldn't be t they be taken away? And if the economy is getting a big boost uh from capital spending and a lot of that's getting debt financed uh and meanwhile personal savings is going down because the baby boomers are retiring and they're not saving. Uh doesn't that mean that the real interest rate the the neutral real interest rate is actually going going up? Um and if the Fed doesn't acknowledge that then the Fed will be too too easy, too stimulative. And what's wrong with that? Well, it uh could be a problem uh to a certain extent for uh price inflation, consumer price inflation. Uh more more inclined to think it could be uh a concern for speculative bubbles. So, lots of lots of interesting topics to think about that are relevant to what we do for a living. Uh but here you can see uh this is a choppy uh uh uh job openings for professional and business services. And uh we're kind of we're encouraged to see it go up. Let's see if it chops back down. But uh we're kind of thinking that uh AI may actually uh be uh uh creating more jobs now than it's taken away. Uh you you don't really see it in this chart without a magnifying glass, but this is looking like it's starting to to at least stop going down. Uh this is another one. This is uh indeed job openings. Uh the share mentioning AI. It's still small at 5.7%. Uh but it's uh gone up a fair amount and uh I guess if we extrapolate this this trend, it's going to go potentially a lot higher. Uh so that's an encouraging sign. Uh we we want to see more job openings reflect some conversation about it would be nice if you knew how to work with AI. Uh construction employment is uh kind of ticking up. It's already at a record high. Maybe there's also a shortage of construction workers. Uh but certainly the while u we're not seeing the kind of employment we might have seen in uh residential housing because it's so weak. Uh we're not seeing a big drop uh like we had in the past when residential took a dive. Instead, we're seeing that it's holding up at a record high. And that's because data centers uh are accounting for uh quite a bit of employment. By the way, uh there's also quite a bit of um uh construction going on of manufacturing facilities generally, not just data centers. Um this is just another manufacturing employment. uh the purchasing managers for manufacturing that employment was below 50 for quite some time. Uh let's see if now it's going to finally crack above 50. Um we suspect it very well might. Uh meanwhile business applications u uh have been very strong and our thinking is that uh could it be that people who are losing jobs in the uh techn some of these technology firms that have talked about layoffs and are actually if uh reducing their payrolls. Could it be that uh some of these people are saying well let's they got rid of our entire group. We were making a lot of progress. we work well together, why don't we start our own firm? And so that uh could be a a result of AI. Uh there is there are inflationary consequences of AI as you can see. PPI electronic components and accessory accessories up 26.9% CPI computer software and accessories up 14.5. Uh PCD computers and peripherals up 1.3. That's kind of surprising that it hasn't hit the consumer, but Apple just recently announced that they were raising their prices and maybe maybe we'll see that there. Um, electricity prices, um, you know, they're not soaring, but they're up 5.9%. People would rather not see the electricity bills going up. Uh, and that's, uh, very well maybe data center related. uh the idea of the real rate going up. Uh as we've said before, baby boomers are retiring. We can see that in social security benefit payments. Uh the number of people has increased significantly. Uh there's uh more fewer foreign workers uh in the United States because of the uh stricter immigration laws. So they're not here doing savings. And so the personal savings rates declining at the same time that we're seeing more debt issuance to finance uh data centers. And so that implies that the real rates going higher. Uh meanwhile, I don't want to get too negative here. I mean, we are after all Yardia Research is the roaring 2020 shop and it's the come the place you come to if you want comfort from the perma bears who are constantly keep trying to keep you out of the market. um the productivity numbers are are just fine and maybe some of that is AI. Look, I think a lot of companies spent the past couple of years uh basically freezing their payrolls uh while they studied AI and and and while they dealt with tariffs and while they dealt with stricter immigration laws. Anyways, they froze their payrolls while they did a kind of toptobottom look at at their businesses particularly from the point of view of AI. And I think after doing that they've concluded that some areas AI could replace workers. Other area are areas AI could very well augment the productivity of workers. In other areas AI had no relevance. And so anyways you put it all together you when you when you kind of go [snorts] through your company like that you could very well increase productivity just by going through the exercise of trying to figure out uh what could use uh some uh more more efficiencies and what you really don't need. Uh, and meanwhile, while the CPI inflation rate, uh, the course PCED inflation rate's going up, as is the CPI, uh, unit labor cost inflation is actually down to 0.5%. Um, Wars said he'd like to use a trimmed mean PCED, uh, which is a 2.41%. I don't know where our our Elias and I have talked about it and we've we wrote about it in the morning briefing. Um, our preference would be a a CPI or a PCED measure that just excludes uh owners occupied rent. It's a nonsensical concept. It's what would owners have to pay in rent to themselves if they were their own landlords. It's a really cockami idea. Uh, and it is above the 2% target. So, it is one of the reasons that inflation is higher than than 2%. Uh let me skip through these. They're kind of related. Uh here you have the consumer prices, less energy, food, and shelter. So uh this is, you know, if we can't take out OE o OED, uh owners occupied rent, uh OEER, um we can still see that uh core inflation excluding shelter uh is going up. Uh and uh this is the same thing for the CPI not going up uh quite as much um for the CPI. This is the housing services uh in um in the in the cons PCED in the consumption deflator. You can see they they've come down substantially. Uh excluding them we have what is called the core uh inflation rate. to services excluding energy and housing and that's ticked up to 3.9%. So there's still an inflation problem out there and we'll get this week will be a big uh week not just for Worsh testifying but also for the inflation news. So another super core uh this kind of looks at the super core inflation rate as well. Um consumer looks great. They're traveling. Um you can see that even though airfares have gone up the TSA travelers checkpoint again if the economy is doing well on the consumer side hanging in there on the employment side then in the dual mandate the Fed's job is to focus on price stability. This is uh US air carrier revenue passenger miles. Uh we've looked at this many times. Every week we look at it. Retail sales are looking just just fine. And this this chart, you all know by now, baby boomers are sitting on a a gold, literally a gold mine of net worth, $89.8 trillion. Um, including the silent generation uh 19. So there's like a hundred trillion dollars in net worth uh amongst sen basically senior citizens. Uh let's have another another look at the a look at the uh market call. We always like to give give you a handle on what's going on with PE time E. Let's focus on E and this one was called great expectations. I just discussed that and you can see great expectations by analysts for 2027 earnings. Uh 2026 is kind of flattened out. Forward earnings time weighted average of these is going up. I mean, if if this just kind of hangs in there at 400, then forward earnings will be at 400 by year end. And 400 time, let's say a PE of 20 is 8,000. We're using 8,000 at 2050 because earnings will probably continue to go up for 2027. So, we might very well get to 4 410 by by the end of the year. Uh this is the quarterly growth rates. Uh so, let's look at the second quarter. That's the earning season that's uh starting now. And you can see that the expectations notched down a bit. Uh but uh we're looking at amazing numbers. 21.6 year-over-year for the second, 21.6 for the third, and uh h let's see if I can't see it, maybe you can't see it either, 26 uh% on a year-over-year basis. Uh so expectations are are great. um some of those expectations these are the sectors uh that we have uh expectations for the second uh quarter and uh the uh this is uh 23.7% on a proforma basis uh technology is a whopper 65.5% uh materials 32.6% 6% um energy up 117 and everything else is kind of garden variety kind of double digits high single digits and then it the only downer is actually healthcare. So what happens if earnings turn out to be kind of what's expected for technology or you know energy is obviously going to float around with what the price of oil is doing. So what happens if uh we get uh we meet expectations here in technology? Will that lead to a sell-off? We saw that with Samsung by the way uh as a as as a sort of a possibility of what could happen. Meanwhile, the earning story looks great. So any uh selloff on disappointments that that earnings weren't even better than expected. Um I think we'll be uh bringing the dip buyers because the earning story is still very strong. Forward earnings S&P 500 uh 400 and 600 all at uh record highs. Uh this is the forward profit margin of uh these u sectors and you can see forward profit margin the S&P 500's been soaring. Uh but you've also seen some good solid increases in the smidcaps. Not yet at record highs, but maybe get getting there. Uh meanwhile, the the Magnificent 7 has had a nice re rebound here. It's still uh lagging for the year, 2.6 versus the X-Mags at 14.3. Um this is another way to look at breadth. the S&P 100 divided by S&P 500. Um, for all the talk about concentration, we we did not so get as concentrated as we did back here during the uh tech bubble and tech wreck. Uh, and maybe if this is about as concentrated as it gets, then we uh that kind of reduces the likelihood of some sort of wreck out out there. Uh, value is uh rising to to a new high. So maybe growth kind of consolidates uh particularly if uh our earnings are only as great as as as was expected uh and not better than than than great. Uh so we may have some stalling out in growth maybe some weakness in growth but the value uh getting that uh being the beneficiary of that smidc the small caps the Russell 1000 looks really good so that's uh another sign of the broadening of the market uh and then you can look at it on your own but uh we're kind of trying to figure out the best way to keep on top of the sectors and industries uh which ones are outperforming uh and which ones are that uh and so you can look at uh these. This is sort of it's a work in progress. So we'll you'll see more of that. Well uh again uh we're not going to have a Q&A because I had to pre-record it. I had another obligation for my Monday call. I hope you have a a great week. Uh it should be action-packed with with wars and with the CPI and PPI and with the straight of our moose. Uh, you got to say there's never a dull moment in our business. Sometimes it would be nice if there if there were, particularly in the summertime when we'd all like to get away to the for the to the beach and just read something that has nothing to do with our business. But, uh, well, with us, uh, we're going to have to stay informed and I hope I can help you do that. Thanks very much. All the best. [music] >> [music] [music]
